question: [File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: checkNetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user flood GET /health/readiness to exhaust the Rosetta server's connection pool, causing the internal rosettaClient.NetworkAPI.NetworkList() call to time out and mark the node as not-ready, thereby masking a concurrent transaction fee manipulation attempt from alerting systems?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: checkNetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user repeatedly call GET /health/readiness to trigger the postgres.New(postgres.Config{DSN: rosettaConfig.Db.GetDsn()}) check and observe timing differences in responses, leaking database connection parameters that could later be used to directly modify fee schedule rows outside of design parameters?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: NewHealthController()] Can an unprivileged external user send a high volume of concurrent GET /health/readiness requests to exhaust the 10-second Timeout budget on the 'postgresql' health.Config check, causing the node to report DOWN and suppress fee-change transaction processing while an attacker submits fee-altering transactions undetected?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: checkNetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that networkList.NetworkIdentifiers[0] is accessed without a bounds check, sending crafted requests that cause NetworkList to return an empty slice, triggering a panic that crashes the readiness handler and prevents fee-schedule integrity checks from running?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: Routes()] Can an unprivileged external user abuse the unauthenticated GET /health/liveness endpoint to perform high-frequency polling, consuming server goroutines and degrading the Rosetta node's ability to validate transaction fee parameters in submitted operations?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: checkNetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user use the response timing of the internal rosettaClient.NetworkAPI.NetworkStatus() call within the readiness check to infer when a fee schedule update has been applied to the network, allowing precise timing of fee-manipulation transactions?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: NewHealthController()] Can an unprivileged external user trigger repeated readiness check failures by saturating the postgres health check connection, causing SkipOnErr: false to mark the service as unavailable and halting fee validation logic that depends on database state?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: checkNetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user observe the error messages logged by log.Errorf('Readiness check, /network/list failed: %v', err) through a side channel or log aggregator to determine internal network topology, enabling targeted fee manipulation by identifying which network identifiers are active?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: checkNetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user craft a scenario where the fmt.Sprintf('http://localhost:%d', port) URL resolves to an attacker-controlled service via DNS rebinding, causing the readiness check to validate against a fake network that reports manipulated fee parameters as legitimate?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: Routes()] Can an unprivileged external user send malformed HTTP requests to GET /health/readiness to trigger unhandled errors in the health.HandlerFunc, causing the readiness endpoint to return inconsistent status codes that confuse orchestration systems and allow fee-modifying transactions to bypass readiness gates?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: checkNetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the absence of rate limiting on GET /health/readiness to perform a timing oracle attack against the rosettaClient.NetworkAPI.NetworkList() response, deducing the current fee schedule version and crafting transactions that exploit the window between fee schedule updates?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: NewHealthController()] Can an unprivileged external user cause the health.New(health.WithChecks(readinessChecks...)) initialization to fail by exhausting system resources before the controller starts, preventing the readiness check from ever running and allowing fee-manipulation transactions to proceed without network validation?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: checkNetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user use the publicly accessible GET /health/readiness endpoint to determine the exact moment the Rosetta node transitions from not-ready to ready after a fee schedule change, timing a fee-manipulation transaction submission to coincide with this transition window?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: checkNetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the rosettaClient is constructed with client.NewConfiguration(serverUrl, 'readiness-check', nil) with no TLS, allowing a man-in-the-middle on the loopback interface to inject a crafted NetworkList response that causes the health check to pass while the node is in a fee-inconsistent state?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: Routes()] Can an unprivileged external user send HTTP/2 or pipelined requests to GET /health/liveness and GET /health/readiness simultaneously to create a race condition in the healthController struct, causing the livenessHealth and readinessHealth handlers to return inconsistent states that mask fee parameter violations?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: checkNetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the 10-second Timeout on the 'network' health.Config check to perform a slow-loris style attack, holding the connection open just under the timeout threshold to prevent the readiness check from completing and blocking fee-schedule integrity validation?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: checkNetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user observe the difference in HTTP response body content between a passing and failing readiness check to infer whether the internal NetworkStatus call succeeded, leaking information about the current network state that could be used to time fee modification attacks?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: Routes()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the lack of authentication on the /health/readiness route to use it as a covert channel for probing the internal state of the Rosetta node's fee processing pipeline, gathering intelligence for a subsequent fee manipulation attack?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: checkNetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user trigger the log.Errorf('Readiness check, /network/status failed: %v', err) path by causing NetworkStatus to fail, and then use the resulting DOWN status to suppress monitoring alerts while submitting transactions with fees outside design parameters?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: checkNetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the unguarded networkList.NetworkIdentifiers[0] index access to cause an out-of-bounds panic when NetworkList returns zero identifiers, crashing the readiness handler goroutine and creating a window where fee-modifying transactions bypass readiness validation?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: NewHealthController()] Can an unprivileged external user send requests that cause the health.New() call to allocate excessive memory for health check state, leading to OOM conditions that degrade the node's ability to enforce fee parameter constraints on incoming transactions?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: checkNetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user use the publicly accessible readiness endpoint to perform binary search timing attacks against the postgres check, determining when fee-related database tables are being written to and timing fee manipulation transactions to coincide with those write windows?
